topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The attic air hung thick and stale, smelling of aged paper and forgotten things. I was supposed to be looking for old photo albums, but my fingers brushed against a dusty cardboard box tucked behind a stack of mildewed linens. Inside, nestled amongst brittle photographs, was a neatly organized collection of every school assignment I had ever turned in. Each piece of paper, from the first-grade spelling tests to my most recent college essays, was there, perfectly preserved. A cold knot tightened in my stomach as I lifted one—a poorly drawn diagram from middle school chemistry. I dropped it instantly, the sound echoing loudly in the silence. My breath hitched, sharp and shallow. I backed away slowly, my palms suddenly slick with sweat, the weight of that meticulous collection pressing down on me like a physical burden.

[story 2]

"Seriously, Mom?" The words tasted like ash in my mouth. I stared at the desk. It wasn't just a pile; it was an archive. Every homework sheet, every test paper, every essay—cataloged and stored with the precision of a librarian. A wave of profound unease washed over me, making my skin prickle. I ran a hand over my face, feeling a sudden, intense need to scrub the grime of the attic off. The sheer volume of documented effort, preserved like artifacts, felt invasive, a testament to a level of control I never understood. I pushed myself back against a nearby trunk, my shoulders tensing up, every muscle vibrating with a restless, unpleasant energy.

[story 3]

I found the box hidden beneath the floorboards in the study. When I lifted the lid, the sight was overwhelming. Hundreds of papers, stacked in pristine order, forming a silent monument to my academic history. My gaze swept over the neat handwriting, the crumpled notes, the graded exams. A strange nausea rose in my throat, making me swallow hard. It wasn't just the quantity; it was the implication—that every single moment of my schooling had been meticulously recorded and kept safe by her. I felt a sudden, sharp urge to recoil, my jaw clenching so tightly my teeth ached. I didn't move to touch anything; I just stood there, rooted to the spot, feeling utterly exposed and profoundly unsettled by the sheer, unbidden history laid bare before me.
